description:
A planetary-science researcher has placed an input file at

        /home/user/data/exoplanets.csv

    and has created (but left empty) an output directory at

        /home/user/output

    Your goal is to help the researcher re-organise the dataset so that it is easier to work with further down the analysis pipeline.  
    Carry out the following three sub-tasks from a Linux terminal.  
    1. Filter the CSV.  
       • Select ONLY the rows whose discoverymethod column is exactly the string Transit (case-sensitive, no leading/trailing spaces).  
       • Preserve the original header row and maintain the original row order.  
       • Save the result to  
            /home/user/output/transit_exoplanets.csv

    2. Produce a one-item JSON summary.  
       • The file must be called  
            /home/user/output/summary.json  
       • It must contain exactly one top-level key named count whose value is the number of rows you wrote to transit_exoplanets.csv (do NOT count the header).  
       • The file must therefore have the following exact byte sequence (including the final newline character at the end of the file):  
            {"count": N}\n  
         where N is the integer row count.

    3. Create a very small plain-text processing log so the researcher can quickly verify what happened.  
       • The log file must be called  
            /home/user/output/process.log  
       • It must consist of exactly two lines, each terminated by a newline character:  
            Line 1:  FILTERED_ROWS: N  
            Line 2:  OUTPUT_FILES: transit_exoplanets.csv summary.json  
       • Replace N with the same integer used in summary.json.

    Notes & verification expectations:  
    • Do NOT modify /home/user/data/exoplanets.csv.  
    • The automated grader will fail the task unless all three new files exist with the precise names and contents described above and are located in /home/user/output.  
    • No extra files should be created in /home/user/output.  
    • All paths are absolute—use them exactly.

    You may use any standard CLI tools available in a typical Debian/Ubuntu environment (e.g., awk, grep, jq, cut, etc.). Two or three concise commands should be enough, but use whatever method you prefer as long as the resulting files meet the strict formatting rules above.
truth:
Pre-existing environment before the agent starts:

    1. Directory /home/user/data already exists (permissions 755).
    2. File /home/user/data/exoplanets.csv (permissions 644) with the exact content:
pl_name,hostname,disc_year,discoverymethod,pl_orbper
"Kepler-22 b","Kepler-22",2011,Transit,289.8623
"HD 209458 b","HD 209458",1999,Transit,3.5247
"51 Pegasi b","51 Pegasi",1995,Radial Velocity,4.2308
"Kepler-10 c","Kepler-10",2011,Transit,45.2943
"GJ 1214 b","GJ 1214",2009,Transit,1.5804

       → When filtered, four data rows match the string Transit.

    3. Directory /home/user/output exists and is empty (permissions 755).

    Expected final state after the agent finishes correctly:

    1. File /home/user/output/transit_exoplanets.csv (permissions 644) exactly:
pl_name,hostname,disc_year,discoverymethod,pl_orbper
"Kepler-22 b","Kepler-22",2011,Transit,289.8623
"HD 209458 b","HD 209458",1999,Transit,3.5247
"Kepler-10 c","Kepler-10",2011,Transit,45.2943
"GJ 1214 b","GJ 1214",2009,Transit,1.5804

    2. File /home/user/output/summary.json (permissions 644) exactly:
{"count": 4}

    (the file must terminate with a single newline character)

    3. File /home/user/output/process.log (permissions 644) exactly:
FILTERED_ROWS: 4
OUTPUT_FILES: transit_exoplanets.csv summary.json

    (the file must terminate with a newline after both lines)

    4. No other files or directories created in /home/user/output.

    Verification checklist for automated tests:
       • Count of rows in transit_exoplanets.csv (excluding header) == 4
       • MD5 or SHA-256 of transit_exoplanets.csv matches the expected content above.
       • summary.json SHA-256 matches the expected one-line JSON with newline.
       • process.log SHA-256 matches the two-line log (including newlines).
